editableNewDialogue.text: Quick sanity check: what’s the name of that animation style where a single line draws a little character that walks along it and the world appears as the line continues?

That style is most commonly associated with **“La Linea”** (Italian: *La Linea*), created by **Osvaldo Cavandoli**. People also describe it as “one-line animation” or “single-line/continuous-line character animation,” but “La Linea style” is the most recognizable label.
Was that originally from a music video or something else?

Originally it was a short animated series/character used in TV segments and commercials (not created for music videos). Later, the look got referenced and reused in other media, including some music-video visuals that mimic the format.
If I wanted to search for modern examples, what keywords would you use?

Try combinations like: **“La Linea style animation”**, **“Cavandoli line character animation”**, **“single line animated character music video”**, **“continuous line doodle animation”**, or **“one-line cartoon character”**. Adding **“music video”** or a genre (e.g., “electronic”) can narrow it further.
Unrelated: I saw a headline about a library lending out unusual items—do you know examples of what some libraries lend besides books?

Some libraries lend out “libraries of things,” like **tool kits**, **seed packets**, **board games**, **musical instruments**, **Wi‑Fi hotspots**, and sometimes even **baking pans** or **museum passes**—it varies a lot by city and funding.
Since you nailed the La Linea thing, I’ve been looping those two again while I do chores—“Bla Bla Bla” on repeat is basically keeping my kitchen moving.
That checks out—those tracks are ridiculously good for keeping momentum. The tight beat and the playful pacing make them perfect background fuel while you’re getting stuff done.
Small correction: I actually *don't* enjoy that Lily Allen “Oh My God” bar video. I mixed it up with another clip—I'm not into that cartoon-woman-in-a-real-bar style at all.

Got it—so the Lily Allen “Oh My God” (cartoon woman in a real-life bar) isn’t one you enjoy, and that mention was a mix-up. The ones you still genuinely like are the La Linea-style animated videos for certain Gigi D’Agostino songs and The Fratellis’ “Whistle for the Choir” with the empty Glasgow night walk.
Yeah—exactly. Keep the Gigi D’Agostino La Linea ones and the Fratellis Glasgow walk, but drop the Lily Allen bar one.
